A sepia-toned photograph shows a horse-drawn delivery wagon parked in front of a storefront. Additional delivery vehicles and storefronts are visible in the background on the street. The photograph appears to be from the early 20th century, documenting a commercial delivery operation.

# Image Description

This is a stereoscopic card (stereograph) containing two nearly identical sepia-toned photographs side by side, labeled with number 58 at the top and 21084 at the bottom. The card is produced by Keystone View Company, with text on the left margin reading "Keystone View Company Producers Consistency Publishers" and on the right margin reading "Manufactured by Keystone View Company, Meadville, Pa. Copyright Enforced."

Both photographs show the same scene: an adult woman in early 20th-century clothing (long dark coat and hat) is feeding ice cream to a small black bear cub standing on its hind legs. The scene takes place outdoors in front of a brick building with mesh or wire material visible on its wall. Vegetation and foliage are visible in the background. The caption at the bottom of the card reads: "Mrs. Herbert Hoover Feeds Ice-Cream To a Small Black Bear Cub, Seward, Alaska."

A grayscale reference chart with graduated tones from white to black is visible at the very top of the image.

# Street Scene in Paris

This black and white photograph shows a Parisian street scene with a group of approximately twenty men gathered in the center of a cobblestone plaza or courtyard. The men are dressed in work clothes and coats typical of the mid-20th century, appearing to be engaged in some form of organized gathering or assembly. A small structure with an umbrella or canopy is visible among the group.

On the left side stands a large Haussmann-style apartment building with classic Parisian architectural features, including ornate stone facades, tall arched windows with metal grillwork, decorative wrought-iron balconies, and folded shutters. A sign reading "COIFFEUR" (hairdresser) is visible on the storefront level.

To the right stands a residential building of different architectural style with a rougher stone facade. Additional buildings and structures are visible in the background at the far right of the frame. The street level is paved with regular stone blocks creating a geometric pattern. A few individuals can be seen standing apart from the main group on both sides of the plaza. The sky appears overcast, typical of Parisian weather photography from this era.

# Urban Destruction Scene with Classical Building

This black and white photograph shows a devastated urban landscape, likely from post-World War II reconstruction. In the foreground, extensive rubble and debris are scattered across the ground, including broken bricks, concrete fragments, and twisted metal. A paved street with visible trolley or streetcar tracks runs across the lower portion of the image. On the left side stands an intact neoclassical building featuring a prominent dome with a geometric latticed framework at its crown, supported by tall Corinthian columns and a classical portico. The dome structure shows detailed architectural ornamentation including a triangular pediment. In contrast, the right and center background show severely damaged brick buildings with collapsed walls, jagged remnants of structures, and partially destroyed facades. Wooden scaffolding and construction materials are visible on the right side. Overhead electrical lines crisscross the scene, and a few pedestrians and what appears to be a cart or wagon can be seen in the middle distance. The sky appears hazy or overcast. At the bottom of the image is a color reference scale used for photographic documentation. The photograph appears to be a historical archival image, mounted on a gray backing.
Collection name: 1906 San Francisco Earthquake and Fire Digital Collection

# Description

This vintage promotional card for Spreckels Rotisserie features an ornate gold-bordered banner at the top identifying the establishment as "A. Wolff prop. 15th Floor Call Bldg San Francisco." The left side displays an illustration of a tall building with a domed clock tower labeled "Cafe," representing San Francisco's iconic Ferry Building. The central and larger portion shows an interior scene of the restaurant with two elegantly dressed diners seated at a table set with wine bottles, glasses, and a decorative centerpiece, gazing out through large windows at an expansive cityscape view of San Francisco's buildings and hills. The bottom of the card reads "Ice Cream Parlor - Cafe and Restaurant 250 Feet Above the Bay." Decorative plant elements frame the lower corners. The publisher information at the top right indicates "Edward H. Mitchell, Publisher, San Francisco." The card uses muted colors including blue, cream, brown, and touches of pink and gold, typical of early 20th-century color printing.
Collection name: Walter Robert and Gail Lynn Kransky collection of Edward H. Mitchell postcards
